storm.summary_layperson: A line of thunderstorms is approaching from the west, but the cool Long Island Sound should weaken them before they reach the cove.
storm.local_effect_blurb: Cool Sound water (70°F) and a 13°F air–water temperature gap create a stable marine layer that typically weakens storms as they cross the Sound.
storm.detail_html:
A pre-frontal squall line is organized across New Jersey and the New York metro area this afternoon, tied to an Enhanced risk of severe weather issued by the NWS for the Lower Hudson Valley. The closest radar echo sits 34 miles WSW of the cove at 39 dBZ, moving eastward at a model-estimated 22 knots (no independent echo velocity is available — storm motion comes from HRRR steering). Best estimate for cove arrival is 5:41 PM, with the uncertainty window running from 5:09 to 6:30 PM. Confidence is low.

The dominant local factor is the cool Long Island Sound. Water is 70°F against an air temperature of 82°F — a 13°F air–water difference that has established a moderate stable marine layer over the Sound. Approaching cells are tracking on a WSW heading, nearly perpendicular to the Sound, so they must cross the full width of cool water before reaching the cove. That geometry consistently starves surface convection of heat and moisture; the NWS Sound-effect assessment rates this scenario high likelihood of weakening.

Bulk 0–6 km wind shear is 40 knots from the west-northwest. Strong shear can help organize cells and sustain them in transit, but it also drives a quick passage rather than a slow-moving training event. HRRR shows 0 J/kg of CAPE locally right now; the OKX forecast discussion cites CAM models at 1,000–2,000 J/kg of MLCAPE ahead of the trough. Even at 2,000 J/kg, CAPE stays below the 3,000 J/kg threshold where cells routinely punch through a marine layer of this strength.

Most likely outcome for the cove: the gradient wind shift from the approaching system is already visible (RYC reading SSE, HRRR calling WNW within the hour), and that transition will continue. Rain is possible, significant thunder less likely here than 2 miles inland. Surrounding communities — Port Chester, White Plains, lower Westchester — carry a higher risk with no marine-layer buffer.

What would upgrade this forecast: a cell reaching 45 dBZ within 25 miles, or an upstream APRS station in Port Chester or Rye reporting a gust ≥35 knots, would move the risk to high. Also note: an Air Quality Alert for smoke is active through Friday evening — visibility is reduced even without precipitation.

arrival.blurb: Radar-tracked cell 34 mi WSW closing at model-estimated 22 kt; the marine layer over the Sound may slow or weaken it, pushing arrival toward the later end of the window.

wind.regime_label: Conflicting winds — light WNW gradient battling a fading sea breeze

hourly.shift_note: sea breeze fading, WNW gradient asserting
WNW gradient establishing, storm approach possible
direction uncertain — ESE post-trough rebound, model confidence low

wind.tactics_blurb: No clear favored side tonight — light, oscillating winds between SSW sea breeze and WNW gradient with a pre-trough lull near 8 PM and an uncertain direction swing after. Stay centered off every start, cover your competitors, and keep one eye on the western horizon for approaching cells.
wind.tactics_detail_html:
Today’s wind falls in the conflicting-winds category — a thermal sea breeze and a light WNW gradient wind are fighting for control of the surface during the race window. The thermal driver is strong: the air–water temperature difference is 12.8°F (Sound water at 70°F, air at 81°F), which normally supports a reliable afternoon sea breeze. Right now at 4:30 PM it is: Riverside YC reads SSE at 4 knots, Bridgeport SSW at 9 knots. But HRRR, which has ingested the approaching frontal system, forecasts WNW for the entire 5–9 PM window — a 93-degree disagreement with current surface observations.

Because the gradient wind is in control (not the sea breeze), the sea-breeze direction correction does not apply to these hourly forecasts. Direction confidence is rated low: the model–obs divergence is too large to reconcile cleanly. The most honest summary is light and oscillating — sea breeze fading between 5 and 6 PM, WNW gradient asserting, then a pre-trough lull around 8 PM, and uncertain post-trough directions after 9 PM.

For the early beats (5–6 PM): the sea breeze (SSE–SSW) may still be competing with the WNW gradient, producing oscillating shifts of 60–90°. No persistent pressure is expected on either tack. Speed will be light — 4–5 knots with gusts to 7–8 — so leverage from position matters more than boat speed gains. Staying centered covers both phases of the oscillation and avoids getting caught in a header on the corner.

Around 7 PM HRRR shows a NW shift at 4–5 knots ahead of the trough passage, followed by a near-calm lull (~2 knots) as the trough itself crosses around 8 PM. After 9 PM the model shows an anomalous ESE swing — treat this as low confidence; post-trough flow after a convective cell is notoriously unpredictable and may oscillate widely before settling.

Sunset is 8:18 PM EDT — well outside the primary race window — so thermal collapse is not a factor here. The main wildcard is the approaching storm line: any cells that reach the cove will produce outflow that can send sudden puffs from the NW or W regardless of the background gradient, so stay nimble and don’t over-commit to a layline.

data_caveats: WLIS buoy (44040) wind obs are 75 minutes old — treated as background reference only
RAP CAPE unavailable; CAPE estimate from OKX AFD discussion (CAMs show 1,000–2,000 J/kg MLCAPE ahead of trough)
HRRR direction and current surface obs diverge by 93° (model WNW, obs SSW); direction confidence low
